_comment: Terrain texture manifest — texture paths and per-texture settings

settings._tiles_per_texture_repeat_comment: How many tiles before grass texture repeats
settings._uv_y_scale_comment: V-axis UV multiplier for isometric foreshortening. Ignored in perspective mode (forced to 1.0)
settings._normal_strength_comment: Normal map intensity multiplier. 0 = flat, 1.0 = subtle, 2.0+ = strong
settings._parallax_strength_comment: Height scale for parallax mapping. 0 = disabled, 0.02-0.08 typical
settings._specular_strength_comment: Intensity of specular highlights (Blinn-Phong). 0 = matte/no highlights, 0.1-0.2 = subtle sheen (dirt, field), 0.3-0.5 = moderate (damp grass, rocks), 0.6-1.0 = glossy/wet look, 1.0+ = strong mirror-like highlights. Roughness from height map G channel modulates per-texel (darker G = rougher = less specular).
settings._anisotropic_filtering_comment: Texture anisotropic filtering level (0 = off, 4/8/16). Reduces blurring at steep view angles
settings._cliff_displacement_strength_comment: World-space magnitude of static geometric displacement on cliff-oriented slots (pushes subdivided vertices along outward normal by height map). 0 = flat cliffs, 0.2 = subtle, 0.4 = default, 0.8+ = pronounced. Only affects slots with cliff_oriented: true.
settings._cliff_displacement_subdivisions_comment: Grid subdivision per cliff face (1 = no subdivision/flat). 4 = 4x4 inner grid = 32 triangles per face. Higher = more geometric detail but more vertices.
settings._plateau_steep_tiles_comment: When true, steep sloped tiles are converted to flat plateaus at tilemap build time so adjacent tiles at different elevations form real vertical cliff faces. Required for the cliff_oriented rock texture to look correct (otherwise UVs streak on tilted tops). Set false to revert to smooth terrain if cliffs look wrong.
settings._plateau_slope_threshold_comment: Corner elevation diff (in tenths, 10 = 1 cell) above which a tile is flattened into a plateau. 14 = 1.4 cells diff, matches the renderer's steep_rocks threshold. Lower = more stepping (more cliffs), higher = fewer but bigger cliffs.
settings._steep_top_subdivisions_comment: TICKET-028: inner-grid subdivision for steep tile top faces. 1 = legacy 2 triangles per tile. 4 = 4×4 inner grid (32 triangles per steep tile). Only tiles with corner-height delta >= steep_top_threshold pay the cost; flat tiles stay at 2 triangles. Bilinear interpolation of corner heights breaks up coarse silhouettes on slopes (river banks, hills).
settings._steep_top_threshold_comment: Corner elevation diff (in tenths, 10 = 1 cell) above which a top face is subdivided. 8 = 0.8 cells covers river bank slopes; 14 matches plateau_slope_threshold for full cliff territory.

rocks._comment: Dirt/rocks blend texture — maps to the 'rocks' terrain blend slot

steep_rocks._comment: Pure rocks texture for steep cliff faces. cliff_oriented=true enables biplanar X+Z sampling, 180° UV rotation, and per-axis reoriented normals (for textures authored as vertical cliff faces).

steep_rocks_02._comment: Second rocks variant, noise-blended with steep_rocks for visual variety

wet_gravel._comment: River-adjacent bank overlay (TICKET-017 Phase 3c). Rendered as a separate pass on `river_adjacent` tiles, layered between roads and water.
wet_gravel._uv_scale_comment: World-space UV multiplier for the bank-overlay texture. Lower = texture tiles over a larger area (coarser pattern); higher = smaller tighter pattern. 0.3 tiles once every ~3.3 world units.

bog._comment: Wet bog terrain (TICKET-020 follow-up). Slice 4 of the packed-layer array. Per-fragment weight comes from a_terrain_id_2.x (second vec4 vertex attribute introduced to extend past the original 4-channel limit).
bog._normal_bias_comment: TICKET-106: bog's normal map has a non-zero average tilt (grass tufts all lean toward one side). Simple sign-flips can only rotate the bias by 90/180°; this 2-vector subtracts the authored average from every per-fragment map_n.xy so the mean lands at zero. Sweep these two numbers (raw tangent units, -1..+1 range) until the Blinn-Phong peak fires when the camera looks toward the sun. Per-pixel surface detail is preserved — only the average shifts. Start with both at 0; the highlight peaks on the wrong side, then nudge x/y in 0.05 increments to find the value where the peak aligns with the sun-facing camera azimuth.
